content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 7, 2007

Mr. Robert Stadum
President Board of Directors
Hi-Desert Water District
225 S. Civic Dr., Ste 1-5
Palm Springs, California 92262-7226

RE:     Your Request for Informal Assistance
        Our File No. I-07-037

Dear Mr. Stadum:

This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions.

QUESTION

Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from participating in a governmental decision involving the Hi-Desert Water District's approval of a Water Supply Assessment ("WSA") report?

CONCLUSION

You are prohibited from participating in the governmental decision if the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of the decision on any of your economic interests are material, as discussed herein. Based on the facts you have provided, we cannot

¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations.

File No. J-07-037
Page No. 2

conclude with certainty that you do or do not have a conflict of interest. Ultimately, the
decision whether to participate in the approval of the WSA is one that must be
determined by you according to your understanding of the facts as they exist at the time
of the governmental decision.

FACTS

On January 16, 2007, we received a request for advice from you concerning other
issues that presented the following facts that are equally applicable to your questions
herein.²

You serve as an elected member of the Board of Directors of the Hi-Desert Water
District (the "District") in the capacity of Board President. The District was formed in
1962 as a special district pursuant to California Water Code sections 3000 et seg. and
provides water services to 25,000 customers within a fifty-seven mile radius that includes
the Town of Yucca Valley, as well as unincorporated areas within the County of San
Bernardino.

You own various real property and business interests within the service area of
the District. You are a fifty-percent owner in Living Space, a real estate investment and
home building company. Living Space has recently purchased a twenty-acre parcel and
an option to purchase an additional forty acres within the service area of the District.
Following the acquisition of the property, including the option, Living Spaces formed
Yucca Valley 98 Homes ("YV98H") a limited liability corporation. You own a twenty-
five percent interest in YV98H. At some future date, YV98H plans to develop the sixty-
acre parcel with residential dwellings.

On February 23, 2007, we received this request via facsimile in which you
presented the following additional facts.

The District has prepared a Final Water Supply Assessment ("Final WSA")
pursuant to California Water Code sections 10910 et seq. (commonly referred to as
Senate Bill 610, "SB 610"). Under this section, once a city or county has determined that
a project is subject to the California Environmental Quality Act, the local municipality
must identify the public water system that may supply water for the project and request a
public water system to prepare a water supply assessment for the project. The WSA must
state "whether the public water system's total projected water supplies available during
normal, single dry, and multiple dry water years during a 20-year projection will meet the
projected water demand associated with the proposed project, in addition to the public
water system's existing and planned future uses, including agricultural and
manufacturing uses." (Cal. Water Code section 10910)(c)(3).) In other words, whether

² The previous request concerned separate governmental decisions unrelated to the question
presented herein. The previous request is being addressed as part of a separate advice letter (07-012).
However, because you indicated that the issue herein was more urgent, we are addressing your second
request first.

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 3

the water supply will be able to meet the projected water demand associated with the
proposed project during the subsequent twenty-year time frame.

The public water system's approval of a WSA does not constitute approval of the
proposed project, nor does the public water system's determination in the water supply
assessment constitute a final decision regarding the sufficiency of water supplies for the
proposed project or other needs of the public water system. Rather, the lead agency (in
the case the Town of Yucca Valley), based on the entire record, shall determine whether
projected water supplies will be sufficient to satisfy the demands of the project, in
addition to existing and planned future uses. (Cal. Water Code section 10911(c).)

The District has recently prepared a WSA in connection with the Town of Yucca
Valley's consideration of a new development, the Mountain Vista Development Project
("Mountain Vista"). The WSA concluded that the total projected water supplies
available to the District, during the next twenty-year period, will be able to meet the
water demand associated with Mountain Vista in addition to the District's existing and
planned future water uses.

As provided in your previous letter, and restated above, you have an ownership
interest in two companies that own a significant amount of property within the District.
None of your properties are within 500 feet of the Mountain Vista Development, and you
state that you have no economic interest in the project.

ANALYSIS
POTENTIAL CONFLICT OF INTEREST

The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform
their duties in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests
or the financial interests of persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).)
Specifically, section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in
making or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a governmental
decision in which the official has a financial interest.

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision, within the
meaning of the Act, when it is "reasonably foreseeable" that the governmental decision
will have a material financial effect on one or more of the public official's economic
interests. (Section 87103; regulation 18700(a).) In order to determine whether a public
official has a disqualifying conflict of interest in a given governmental decision, the
Commission has adopted an eight-step analytical framework. (Regulation 18700(b)(1)-
(8).) The general rule, however, is that a conflict of interest exists whenever a public
official makes a governmental decision that has a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on one or more of his or her financial interests. (Section 87103.)

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 4

Step One: Are You a Public Official?

The conflict of interest provisions of the Act only apply to public officials.
(Section 87100.) Public officials include members of committees, boards or
commissions with decision making authority. (Regulation 18701(a)(1).) Officials of all
special purpose districts are also included under the provisions of the rule. As a member
of the Hi-Desert Water District you are a public official under the Act.

Step Two: Will You be Making, Participating in Making, or Influencing a Governmental
Decision?

A public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the purview of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.1.)

In addition, a public official "participates in making" a governmental decision
when he or she, without substantive review, negotiates, advises, or makes
recommendations regarding a decision. (Regulation 18702.2.) Therefore, you will be
participating in making a governmental decision if you advise, recommend or negotiate a
course of action for the District pertaining to the District's approval of a Water Supply
Assessment ("WSA") report.³

Furthermore, a public official is "influencing a governmental decision" if he or
she contacts, or appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence any member, officer,
employee, or consultant of the District regarding the decisions. (Regulation 18702.3.)

As a boardmember of the District you will be called upon to consider whether the
District should approve or disapprove a Water Supply Assessment ("WSA") report.
Therefore, you will be making, participating in making, or otherwise using your official
position to influence a governmental decision.

Step Three: What are Your Economic Interests—The Possible Sources of Conflict of
Interest?

A public official has a "financial interest" in a governmental decision if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or
her immediate family, or on any one of five enumerated economic interests. (Section
87103; regulations 18703-18703.5.) The applicable economic interests include:

³ Exception: Making or participating in a governmental decision does not include appearances by a public
official as a member of the general public before an agency in the course of its prescribed governmental
function to represent himself or herself on matters related solely to the official's wholly owned real
property or business entity. (See regulation 18702.4) This exception does not apply to you because you do
not have a financial interest in Mountain Vista and have not indicated that you want to speak on this
matter as a member the general public.

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 5

• an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she has a direct or indirect
investment of $2,000 or more (section 87103(a); regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which
he or she is a director, officer, partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of
management (section 87103(d); regulation 18703.1(b));

• an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a direct or indirect
interest of $2,000 or more (section 87103(b); regulation 18703.2);
• an economic interest in any source of income, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(c);
regulation 18703.3);

• an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if the gifts aggregate to $390
or more within 12 months prior to the decision (section 87103(e); regulation
18703.4);

• A public official has an economic interest in his or her personal expenses, income,
assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. This is also
knows as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, regulation 18703.5)

For the purposes of this section indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity in which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103(e).)

Economic Interests Identified

Based on the facts submitted, you have identified the following economic
interests:

Business Entities:

You have an economic interest in the business entity Living Space both because
you have an investment of $2,000 or more and because you are a partner in the business.
Additionally, you have an economic interest in the business entity YV98H because you
indicated that your twenty-five percent ownership interest in YV98H represents an
investment of $2,000 or more. If you are an officer, director, partner, employee, or hold
any position of management in YV98H, you would also have an economic interest in the
company based on your position. (See regulation 18703.1(b).)

Moreover, the Act provides that an indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a ten-percent interest or greater. (Section 87103.) Because you
own a twenty-five percent interest in YV98H and a fifty-percent interest in Living Space,
you have an indirect interest in any investment or interest owned by either of these two
business entities.

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 6

Interest in Real Property:

Interest in real property of an individual includes a "pro rata share of interests in
real property of any business entity or trust in which the individual … owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, a ten-percent interest or greater." (Section 82033.) Because
you own a twenty-five-percent interest in Living Space and a fifty-percent interest in
YV98H, you have an economic interest in the real property held by those business
entities.

Sources of Income:

An official has an economic interest in any person from whom he or she has
received income aggregating $500 or more within 12 months prior to the time when the
relevant decision is made. (Section 87103(c); regulation 18703.3(a)(1).) Additionally,
income of an individual includes a pro rata share of any income of any business entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse owns, directly, indirectly, or beneficially, a 10-
percent interest or greater. Accordingly, in addition to the possible economic interests in
your business entities as a source of income to you, you would also have an economic
interest in any individual who provided income to the businesses if your pro rata share of
that income equals $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision.

You have not provided any facts indicating the amount of income you may have
received from any source. For purposes of our analysis, we will assume you have a
source of income from your economic interests in each of your businesses. Because you
have not indicated any other sources of income, including a pro rata share of the business
income from any customers of the business, our analysis is limited to the source of
income economic interests from your businesses.

Please note that should you sell a home or property related to the development,
from which you receive $500 or more within 12 months of any governmental decision
you will need to determine whether any of the buyers qualify as a source of income and
whether that may lead to a potential conflict of interest in the decision.

Because you have not provided any information regarding other economic
interests you may have, our analysis is limited to your economic interest in your business
entities, including the real property holding of those businesses.

Step Four: Are Your Economic Interests Directly or Indirectly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?

Business Entity, Source of Income:

A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, are
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or indirectly or by an agent:

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 7

"(1) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) Is a named party in,
or is the subject of, the proceeding concerning the decision before the
official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if
a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlement to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Section 87103; Regulation 18704.1(a).)

Since it appears that your company did not initiate the WSA nor is your company
a named party in the proceeding concerning the WSA, your business interests are not
directly involved in the decision to approve the WSA.

Interests in Real Property

Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved (subject to some exceptions) if any of the following apply:

• The property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(1).)

• The governmental decision involves a zoning or rezoning, annexation or de-
annexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city,
county, district, or other local governmental subdivision of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the real property.
(Regulation 18704.2(a)(2).)

• The governmental decision involves the issuance, denial, or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlement authorizing a specific use of the real
property in which the official has an interest. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(3).)

• The governmental decision involves the imposition, repeal or modification of any
taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official has an
interest. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(4).)

• The governmental decision is to designate the survey area, to select the project
area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to certify the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above decisions; and real
property in which the official has an interest or any part of it is located within the
boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the redevelopment area. (Regulation
18704.2(a)(5).)

File No. J-07-037
Page No. 8

• The decision involves construction of or improvements to streets, water, sewer, storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has an interest will receive new or improved services. (Regulation 18704.2(a)(6).)

According to your facts, your real property is not within 500 feet of the Mountain Vista development and the decision to approve the WSA does not involve any other factors listed above as far as an impact on any property you own, as your property is not included in the boundaries, or subject matter, of the governmental decision as described by the WSA.

For these reasons, your real estate interests are not directly involved in the decision to approve the WSA.

Step Five: Applicable Materiality Standard

Business Entities/Sources of Income

You have not provided detailed information about the finances for YV98H or Living Space, but assuming that your business is not publicly traded, for a business entity that is not directly involved in a governmental decision the materiality standard of regulation 18705.1(c) applies. Pursuant to this materiality standard, the financial effect of a government decision on a small business is material if it is reasonably foreseeable that:

"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of $20,000 or more; or,

"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating existing expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,

"(C) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of $20,000 or more." (Reg. 18705.1(c)(4).)"

Because we do not have any details regarding what the financial effects of the governmental decision will be, if any, on any of the above identified economic interests, we cannot determine whether the governmental decision regarding Mountain Vista will or will not have a material effect on your economic interests.

Real Property

"The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to be material."

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 9

(Regulation 18705.2(b)(1).) Therefore, it is presumed that the financial effect of the
governmental decision regarding Mountain Vista will not be material when analyzing
your identified economic interests in real property.

However, please be aware that if any single economic interest identified by a
public official is presumed to be materially affected by a governmental decision, the
governmental decision will be presumed to have a material effect on the public official.
This is the case even if it has been determined that the effect of a governmental decision
on several other economic interests will not be material.

Step Six: Reasonable Foreseeability

Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a governmental decision is made depends on the facts of each particular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" means "substantially likely." (Regulation 18706;
In re Thorner (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 198.) A financial effect need not be a certainty to be
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibid.) Please note that the Commission does not act as a finder
of fact when providing advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

According to our understanding of the facts you presented and the application of
Water Code section 10910, your determination must be based upon whether it is or is not
reasonably foreseeable that the decision to approve the WSA will have a material impact
on any of your economic interests. While the WSA does conclude that total projected
water available to the District during the next 20-year period will meet the
projected demands of Mountain Vista and the District's existing and "planned future
uses," it is for you to determine whether this finding has any impact on your tentative
plans to construct residential developments. In other words, if the consequences of the
WSA for the Mountain Vista project, make it reasonably
foreseeable that your own future plans to build housing developments may be materially
affected, (for example: if the approval of the WSA would make it more or less likely that
the water assessment needs of your future developments would be met), you would be
prohibited from participating in the decision.

That being said, the concept of material foreseeability is heavily driven by the
particular facts of each situation as they exist at the time the decision is made. Therefore,
the determination of whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality
standard will be met for your economic interests is necessarily a factual question that is
ultimately one for you to make based on your understanding of the facts. Additionally,
the determination must be made for each decision under the facts present at the time of
the decision. Accordingly, even if you determine that there will be no material financial
effect resulting from the decision to approve the WSA in this case, that determination
will again need to be made with respect to any future reports involving the water
assessment needs of the District.

File No. I-07-037
Page No. 10

Steps Seven and Eight. The "Public Generally" and "Legally Required Participation"
Exceptions.

An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the "public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantially the
same effect on a significant segment of the public. (Section 87103; regulation
18707(a).) Your account of the facts does not suggest that the "public generally"
exception is available to you.

An official with a conflict of interest may still participate in the decision under the
"legally required participation" exception. (Regulation 18708(a).) This is an exception
that typically applies when an agency is unable to assemble a quorum of its members
without participation of an official who has a conflict of interest. You have not suggested
that this exception might become applicable in this case.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel

By: Melissa Scolari
Legal Intern, Legal Division

assets, or liabilities, as well as those of his or her immediate family. fhis is atso
knows as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103, regulation Ig703.5.)
For the purposes of this section indirect investment or interest means any
investment or interest owned by a business entity in which the official owns directly,
indirectly, or beneficially a 10-percent interest oi greater. (section szror(e)i
Economic Interests Identified
Based on the facts submitted, you have identified the following economic
interests:
Business Entities:
You have an economic interest in the business entity Living Space both because
you have an investment of $2,000 or more and because you are a partner in the business.
Additionally, you have an economic interest in the business entity fvlgH because you
indicated that your twenty-five percent ownership interest in yvisrr represents an
investment of $2,000 ormore. If you are an officer, director, partner, employee, orhold
anyposition of management in YV98H, you would also have an economic interest in the
company based on your position. (See regulation I g703. I O).)
Moreover, the Act provides that an indirect investment or interest mearrs any
investment or interest owned by a business entity which the official owns directlS
indirectly, or benefici ally a ten-percent interest or greater. (Section g7103.) Because you
own a twenty-five-percent interest in YV98H and a fifty-percent interest inLiving Space,
you have an indirect interest in any investment or interesiowned by either of these two
business entities.
Interest in Real Property:
Interest in real property of an individual includes a "pro rata share ofinterests in
real property of a1Y business entity or trust in which the individual ... owns, directly,
indirectly or beneficially, aten-percent interest or greater." (Section g2033) necause
you own a twenty-five-percent interest in Living Space and a fifty-percent interest in
YV98H, you have an economic interest in the real property held 6y tno." business
entities.
Sources oflncome:
An official has an economic interest in anyperson from whom he or she has
received income aggregating $500 ormore within i2 months prior to the time when the
relevant decision is made. (Section 87103(c); regulation tSZOi.:1a;(l).) Additionally,
income of an individual includes a pro rata share of any incom" oi*i U"siness entity or
trust in which the individual or spouse- owns, directly, indirectly, orbeneficially, a l0-
percent interest or greater. Accordingly, in addition to the posibl" economic interests in
your business entities as a source of income to you, you would also have an economic
interest in any individual who provided income to the businesses if your pro rata share of
that income equals $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. 
^
You have not provided *y facts indicating the amount of income you may have
received from any source. Forpurposes of our analysis, we will assume ylu have a
source of income from your economic interests in each of your businessel. Because you
have not indicated any other sources of income, including apro ratashme of the business
income from any customers of the business, our analysisls fimited to the source of
income economic interests from your businesses.
Please note that should you sell a home or property related to the development,
from which you receive $500 or more within 12 mbnths of any govemmental decision
you will need to determine whether any of the buyers qualifu ur-u *our"" of income and
whether that may lead to a potential conflict of interesfin thl decision.
Because you have not provided any information regarding other economic
interests you may have, our analysis is limited to your economicinterest in your business
entities, including the real property hording of those businesses.
Step Fow: Are YourEconomic Interests Direcfly or Indirecfly Involved in the
Governmental Decision?
Business Entity, Source oflncome:
A person, including business entities, sources of income, and sources of gifts, are
directly involved in a decision before an official's agency when that person, either
directly or indirectly or by an agent:
"(l) Initiates the proceeding in which the decision will be made by filing
an application, claim, appeal, or similar request or; (2) Is a named parry in,
or is the subject o{ the proceeding conceming the decision before ihe
official or the official's agency. A person is the subject of a proceeding if
a decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation
of any license, permit, or other entitlernent to, or contract with, the subject
person." (Section 87 I O3 ; Regulation I 870a. I (a).)
Since it appears that your company did not initiate the WSA nor is your company
a named party in the proceeding concerning the WSA, your business interests are not
directly involved in the decision to approve the WSA.
Interests in Real Property
Real property in which a public official has an economic interest is directly
involved (subject to some exceptions) if any ofthe folrowing apply:
o The property is located within 500 feet of the boundaries of the property which is
the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulati on lg7o4.2t"Xrl.l 
-
o The govemmental decision involves azoringor rezoning, annexation or de-
annexation, sale, purchase, or lease, or inclusion in or exclusion from any city,
county, district, or other local governmental subdivision of the real property in
which the official has an interest or a similar decision affecting the iealproperty.
(Regul atio n I 87 0 4.2(a)Q).)
o The govemmental decision involves the issuance, denial, or revocation of a
license, permit or other land use entitlernent authorizing a specific use of the real
property in which the official has an interest. (Regulation tg7u.2(a)(3).)
o The govemmental decision involves the imposition, repeal ormodification of any
taxes or fees assessed or imposed on the real property in which the official has an
interest. (Regulatio n 1 87 04.2(a)(4).)
o The governmental decision is to designate the suryey area, to select the project
area, to adopt the preliminary plan, to form a project area committee, to-certiff the
environmental document, to adopt the redevelopment plan, to add territory to the
redevelopment area, or to rescind or amend any of the above decisions: and real
property in which the official has an interest or any part of it is located within the
boundaries or the proposed boundaries of the redevelopment area. (Regulation
1870a.2@)(s).)
o The decision involves construction of or improvements to streets, water, sewer,
storm drainage or similar facilities, and the real property in which the official has
an interest will receive new or improved seryices. (Regulation 1S7Oa.2@)(6).)
According to your facts, your real property is not within 500 feet of the Mountain
Vista development and the decision to approve the WSA does not involve any other
factors listed above as far as an impact on any property you own, as your property is not
included in the boundaries, or subject matter, of the gor".n**tal decision as described
by the WSA.
For these reasons, your real estate interests are not directly involved in the
decision to approve the WSA.
Step Five: Applicable Materiatity Standard
Business Entities/Sources of Income
You have not provided detailed information about the finances for yVggH or
Living Space, but assuming that your business is not publicly traded, for a business entity
that is not directly involved in a governmental decision the materiality standard of
regulation 18705.1(c) applies. Pursuant to this materiality standard, tire financial effect
of a government decision on a small business is material if it ir reasonably foreseeable
that:
"(A) The governmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the business entity's gross revenues for a fiscal year in the amount of
$20,000 or more; or,
"(B) The governmental decision will result in the business entity incurring
or avoiding additional expenses or reducing or eliminating exisiing
expenses for a fiscal year in the amount of $5,000 or more; or,
"(C) The govemmental decision will result in an increase or decrease in
the value of the business entity's assets or liabilities of
$20,000 or more." (Reg. I 8705.1(c) (4).)-
Because we do not have any details regmding what the financial effects of
the governmental decision will be, if any, on any of ihe above identified economic
interests, we cannot determine whether the govemmental decision regarding
Mountain Vista will or will not have a material effect on your economic interests.
Real Property
"The financial effect of a governmental decision on real property which is
indirectly involved in the governmental decision is presumed not to b" material.,,


FileNo. I-07-037
(Regulation 18705.2@)(l).) Therefore, it is presumed that the financial effect of the
govemmental decision regarding Mountain Vista will not be material when analyzing
your identified economic interests in real.property.
However, please be aware that if any single economic interest identified by a
public official is presumed to be materially affected by a governmental decision, tire
governmental decision will be presumed to have a material effect on the public official.
This is the case even if it has been determined that the effect of a govemirental decision
on several other economic interests will not be material.
Step Six: Reasonable Foreseeability
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably foreseeable at
the time a govemmental decision is made depends on the facts of eachparticular case.
As used here, "reasonably foreseeable" mears "substantially likely.,, (Regulation 1g706;
InreThorner(1975) I FPPCops. 19s.) AfinancialeffectneednotbeaIertaintytobe
considered reasonably foreseeable; a substantial likelihood that it will occur suffices to
meet the standard. On the other hand, if an effect is only a mere possibility, it is not
reasonably foreseeable. (Ibtd.) Please note that the Commission-does not'actas a finder
of fact when providing advice. (In re oglesby (1975) I Fppc ops. 71.)
According to our understanding of the facts you presented and the application of
Water Code section 10910, your determination mmi b" based upon wheth"" it i. or is not
reasonably foreseeable that the decision to approve the WSA *ill hur" a material impact
on any of your economic interests. While the WSA does conclude that total projected
water supplies available to the District during the next 2O-yearperiod will meet the
projected demands of Mountain Vista andtheDistrict's existing and..planned future
uses," it is for you to determine whether this finding has any impact ori yorr. tentative
plans to construct residential developments. In other wordr, if til" 
"onr"qrr"rr"es of the
lPProval or disapproval of the WSA for the Mountain Vistaproject, make it reasonably
foreseeable that youl own future plans to build housing deviopments maybe materially
affected, (for example: if the approval of the wSA *o.la maki it more oil"r.likely that
the water assessment needs of your future developments would be met), you would be
prohibited from participating in the decision.
That being said, the concept of material foreseeability is heavily driven by the
particular facts of each situation as they exist at the time the decision is made. Tirerefore,
the determination of whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the applicable materiality
standard will be met for your economic interests is necessarily a A"iuuf question that is
ultimately one for you to make based on your understanding of the facts. Additionally,
the determination must be made for each decision under the facts present at the time of
the decision- Accordingly, even if you determine that there will be no material financial
effect resulting from the decision to approve the WSA in this case, that determination
will again need to b-e made with respect to any future reports invoiving the water
assessment needs of the District.


PageNo. l0
steps Seven and Eight The "Public Generally' and "Legally Required participation,
Exceptions.
An official who otherwise has a conflict of interest in a decision may still
participate under the-"public generally" exception. This exception applies when the
financial effect of a decision on a public official's economic interests is substantialy the
same as the effect on a significant segment of the public. (Section g7103; regulation
18707(a).) Your account of the facts does not suggest thaithe ..public g*"ritty,
exception is available to you.
An official with a conflict of interest may still participate in the decision under the
"legally required participation" exception. (Regulation tszosla;.) This is an exception
that tlpically applies when * ug"o"y is unable to assernble a quorum of its members
without participation of an official who has a conflict ofinterest. you have not suggested
that this exception might become applicable in this case.

embedding.first_500_words: FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 (916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 March 7, 2007 Mr. Robert Stadum President Board of Directors Hi-Desert Water District 225 S. Civic Dr., Ste 1-5 Palm Springs, California 92262-7226 RE: Your Request for Informal Assistance Our File No. I-07-037 Dear Mr. Stadum: This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as the finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.) Please note, the Commission will not advise with respect to past conduct. (Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Therefore nothing in this letter should be construed to evaluate any conduct that may have already taken place, and any conclusions contained in this letter apply only to prospective actions. QUESTION Do the Act's conflict of interest provisions prohibit you from participating in a governmental decision involving the Hi-Desert Water District's approval of a Water Supply Assessment ("WSA") report? CONCLUSION You are prohibited from participating in the governmental decision if the reasonably foreseeable financial effects of the decision on any of your economic interests are material, as discussed herein. Based on the facts you have provided, we cannot ¹ Government Code sections 81000-91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109-18997, of the California Code of Regulations. File No. J-07-037 Page No. 2 conclude with certainty that you do or do not have a conflict of interest. Ultimately, the decision whether to participate in the approval of the WSA is one that must be determined by you according to your understanding of the facts as they exist at the time of the governmental decision. FACTS On January 16, 2007, we received a request for advice from you concerning other issues that presented the following facts that are equally applicable to your questions herein.² You serve as an elected member of the Board of Directors of the Hi-Desert Water District (the "District") in the capacity of Board President. The District was formed in 1962 as a special district pursuant to California Water Code sections 3000 et seg. and provides water services to 25,000 customers within a fifty-seven mile radius that includes the Town of Yucca Valley, as well as unincorporated areas within the County of San Bernardino. You own various real property and business interests within the service area of the District. You are a fifty-percent owner in Living Space, a real estate investment and home building company. Living Space has recently purchased a twenty-acre parcel and an option to purchase an additional forty acres within the service area of the District. Following the acquisition of the property, including the option, Living Spaces formed Yucca Valley 98 Homes ("YV98H") a limited liability corporation. You own a twenty- five percent interest in YV98H. At some future date, YV98H plans to develop the sixty- acre
